name: Bahraini Arabic

ancestors.notes: Gulf Arabic base — inherits *q→[ɡ], velar affrication, interdental retention and emphatic spreading.
Persian adstrate: Baḥārna (Shia) community historical ties; /p/ in some older loanwords predating English contact.

notes: Bahraini Arabic. Bahrain has a well-known DIALECT SPLIT along communal lines (Holes' long-term work on Eastern Arabia): the sedentary Shia Baḥārna vs the Bedouin-descended Sunni ʕArab. This single spec models the GENERAL Gulf pattern (inherited from ar-x-gulf): *q → [ɡ]; velar affrication /k/→[tʃ], /ɡ/→[dʒ] before high front /i, iː/; interdentals retained. NOTE ON THE SPLIT (not separately modelled here, as one spec = one lect): the Sunni ʕArab variety is Bedouin-type and patterns like mainstream Gulf (*q→[ɡ], affrication). The sedentary Baḥārna (Shia) variety is more conservative — historically it can retain /q/ as [q] in some items, shows a distinct jiim reflex, and carries the older Persian adstrate — so applying the *q→[ɡ] and affrication defaults over-generalises for Baḥārna speech. Persian /p/ in some vocabulary predates English contact. Input contract: fully diacritised (tashkeel) Arabic.

sources.author: Alshammari, K. K.
sources.title: An Optimality-Theoretic Account of Velar Affrication in Northern Najdi Arabic
sources.publisher: Journal of Language Teaching and Research 17(4)
sources.notes: Read in full (open-access PDF). Literature review (p.1335) of Gulf/Eastern-Arabian velar affrication and *q reflexes supporting the inherited GULF_K/G_AFFRICATION rules and *q→[ɡ].

sources.author: Al-Balushi, R.
sources.title: Omani Arabic: More than a Dialect
sources.notes: Read the phonology sections. Documents the Eastern-Arabian sedentary-vs-Bedouin dialect typology (after Holes) that underlies the Bahraini Baḥārna/ʕArab split, and the range of *q reflexes across those types (p.87).

location.notes: Manama. A national variety within the Gulf continuum. [Wikidata Q3882 P625]
